slides.title: Every Project Gets Its Own Sandbox
slides.body: Two projects can need **different versions of the same package** — and global installs can only hold one. Virtual environments give each project its own private space so versions never clash.
slides.tip: Think of it like phone apps: Instagram and WhatsApp each run in their own sandbox and can never break each other.

slides.title: Create It Once
slides.body: Python's built-in `venv` tool creates a `.venv` folder inside your project. Run this **once per project** — it only takes a few seconds.
slides.code:
# Run in your terminal, inside your project folder
python -m venv .venv
slides.tip: The dot in `.venv` makes the folder hidden on macOS/Linux — just a tidy convention.

slides.title: Activate Before You Install
slides.body: Creating the environment isn't enough — you must **activate** it so `pip` knows to install packages inside `.venv`, not globally.
slides.tip: Your prompt will show (.venv) when active — always check for it before running pip.

slides.title: Install and Freeze Your Deps
slides.body: With the environment active, `pip install` lands packages inside `.venv` only. Save the exact versions to share with teammates.
slides.code:
pip install requests
pip freeze > requirements.txt
slides.output:
# requirements.txt gets created with pinned versions:
# requests==2.32.3
# certifi==2024.2.2
# ...
slides.tip: Commit requirements.txt to Git — never commit the .venv folder itself.

slides.title: You've Got It!
slides.body: Create once, activate every session, install freely, freeze before sharing. Virtual environments are a **core professional habit** — every Python developer uses them.
slides.code:
# The whole workflow in five steps
python -m venv .venv          # 1. create
source .venv/bin/activate     # 2. activate
pip install requests          # 3. install
pip freeze > requirements.txt # 4. save deps
deactivate                    # 5. done for today
